questions.question: List any three abiotic components of an ecosystem. (2 marks)
expectedPoints: Any three from: Sunlight, Temperature, Water, Soil minerals, Atmospheric gases, Wind.

questions.question: Explain the role of decomposers in an ecosystem. Why are they essential? (3 marks)
expectedPoints: They break down complex dead organic matter (detritus) from all trophic levels.
They convert this matter into simpler inorganic substances (nutrients).
They release these nutrients back into the soil/environment, making them available for producers, thus closing the nutrient cycle.

questions.question: Differentiate between the flow of energy and the cycling of nutrients in an ecosystem. (5 marks)
expectedPoints: Energy Flow: It is unidirectional and linear, moving from the sun to producers and then through various trophic levels.
Energy is not recycled; a large portion is lost as heat at each transfer according to the 10% law.
Nutrient Cycling: It is cyclic and involves the movement of elements like carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus.
Nutrients are recycled from abiotic to biotic components and back again through processes like decomposition.
Conclusion: Energy flows through the system and is lost, while nutrients are constantly recycled within the system.

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, describe a food web. How is it more realistic than a food chain? (5 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: A food web is a network of interconnected food chains.
It shows multiple feeding relationships and the complexity of interactions.
Realism: Most organisms have multiple food sources and are preyed upon by multiple predators. A single food chain oversimplifies this.
Stability: Food webs provide stability to an ecosystem. The removal or decline of one species has a diluted effect as others can take its place in the chain.
Diagram: A student should draw a basic web with at least 8 organisms across 3 trophic levels, showing multiple connections.
